enrichment.short_description: Slite is an AI-powered team knowledge base and wiki platform that helps companies write, organize, verify, and instantly retrieve internal documentation.
enrichment.starting_price: $10 per user per month (Basic plan, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Slite Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Slite offers in 2026: AI knowledge base features, Basic, Pro, and Enterprise pricing, pros and cons, and whether it fits your team's docs workflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Slite is a team knowledge base and internal wiki platform founded in 2016 by Christophe Pasquier and headquartered in Paris, France. The company, which went through Y Combinator, has raised roughly 15.5 million dollars from investors including Index Ventures and Spark Capital, and employs an estimated 30 to 40 people. Slite positions itself as a self-maintaining, AI-powered knowledge base: teams write docs in a collaborative editor that supports rich formatting, embedded video, diagrams, and files up to 200MB, while an AI assistant called Ask answers employee questions directly from verified knowledge base content instead of making them search manually. A newer Slite Agent tier can also search across connected tools such as Slack, Google Drive, GitHub, Jira, Linear, HubSpot, Salesforce, and Intercom to surface answers from outside the wiki itself. Slite includes a doc verification and fact-checking workflow so admins can flag outdated pages and keep company information trustworthy over time. Pricing is tiered per user per month, starting with a Basic plan for straightforward documentation and search, moving up to a Pro plan that unlocks the AI agent, cross-tool search, and single sign-on, and topping out with a custom Enterprise plan for larger organizations that need audit logs, SCIM provisioning, and dedicated support. Typical customers are small to mid-sized teams in engineering, HR, operations, and sales who need a fast, searchable home for onboarding guides, policies, meeting notes, and workflows, and Slite counts companies like Airbnb and WeTransfer among its client base.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Slite
paragraphs: Slite is a cloud-based knowledge management and team wiki tool designed to be the single source of truth for a growing company's internal information. Rather than treating documentation as a static archive, Slite frames itself as a self-maintaining knowledge base: content is written collaboratively, verified on a schedule, and made instantly answerable through an AI assistant, so teams spend less time hunting through old Slack threads or forgotten Google Docs.

The platform was built for cross-functional teams such as engineering, HR, operations, and sales, who use it for onboarding guides, internal policies, meeting notes, and process documentation. Because Slite emphasizes searchability and trust in the content, it is often positioned against tools like Notion and Confluence for teams that specifically want a lightweight, focused knowledge base rather than a general-purpose workspace.

Slite's collaborative editor supports standard rich text formatting alongside embedded video, diagrams, and large file uploads, and documents can be organized into channels or collections so that departments can maintain their own areas of the knowledge base while still being searchable company-wide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The centerpiece of Slite's product is Ask, an AI assistant that answers employee questions in natural language by pulling directly from verified content inside the knowledge base, reducing the time spent searching for information manually. On higher tiers, this expands into a broader Slite Agent that can also search connected third-party tools such as Slack, Jira, Linear, GitHub, Google Drive, Salesforce, HubSpot, and Intercom, surfacing answers that live outside the wiki itself.

Slite also includes a doc verification workflow that helps teams keep information current: pages can be flagged for review, marked as verified, or fact-checked with suggested fixes so that outdated policies do not silently mislead employees. A Knowledge Management Panel gives admins visibility into which docs exist, which are stale, and where gaps might be forming.

Beyond AI and verification, Slite supports MCP and API access for custom integrations, OpenID single sign-on for centralized authentication, custom domains for publishing docs externally, and reader-only seats so companies can share knowledge with people who do not need full editing rights.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Slite uses per-user, per-month pricing billed annually across three tiers. The Basic plan, at 10 dollars per user per month, covers unlimited docs, AI search and answers, the doc verification workflow, the Knowledge Management Panel, and MCP and API access, making it a reasonable entry point for smaller teams that mainly need a well-organized, searchable wiki.

The Pro plan, at 20 dollars per user per month, adds the full Slite Agent with cross-tool search, doc fact-checking with suggested fixes, agent workflows, a pool of monthly AI credits per seat, OpenID single sign-on, and a custom domain for publicly published docs. This is the tier most growing companies choose once they want the knowledge base to actively connect with the rest of their software stack.

Enterprise pricing is custom and adds reader-only seats, advanced security controls, audit logs, a service level agreement, a dedicated account manager, priority support, custom agent sources, and SCIM provisioning, plus migration and onboarding assistance for larger, more security-conscious organizations.

features.title: AI-powered Ask assistant
features.description: Employees can ask questions in plain language and get answers pulled directly from verified knowledge base content instead of manually searching documents.

features.title: Cross-tool Slite Agent
features.description: On the Pro tier, the agent can search connected apps like Slack, Google Drive, GitHub, Jira, Linear, HubSpot, and Salesforce to surface answers beyond the wiki.

features.title: Doc verification and fact-checking
features.description: Pages can be reviewed, marked verified, or flagged with suggested fixes so outdated information does not quietly stay live.

features.title: Collaborative rich-text editor
features.description: Supports formatting, embedded video and diagrams, and file uploads up to 200MB for building detailed internal documentation.

features.title: Knowledge Management Panel
features.description: Gives admins visibility into the state of the knowledge base, including stale or unverified content that needs attention.

features.title: MCP and API access
features.description: Allows teams to connect Slite to custom workflows and tools beyond the built-in integrations.

features.title: Single sign-on and reader seats
features.description: OpenID SSO and reader-only seats support broader, more controlled distribution of company knowledge on higher tiers.

features.title: Custom domain publishing
features.description: Docs can be published externally under a custom domain, useful for public help centers or customer-facing knowledge bases.

pros: AI assistant answers questions directly from verified content rather than returning a list of links to search through
Doc verification workflow helps keep a growing knowledge base accurate over time instead of going stale
Clean, focused editor that is easy for non-technical team members to adopt quickly
Cross-tool search on the Pro plan connects the wiki to tools teams already use daily
cons: Basic plan does not include the full AI agent or cross-tool search, which are the platform's most distinctive features
Per-seat pricing can get expensive for larger organizations compared to flat-rate wiki tools
Smaller team size and funding relative to competitors like Notion or Confluence may raise long-term platform risk questions for some buyers
Less suited to teams that want an all-in-one workspace with tasks, databases, and project management alongside docs

faqs.question: What is Slite used for
faqs.answer: Slite is used as a team knowledge base and internal wiki for storing and instantly retrieving company information such as onboarding guides, policies, meeting notes, and process documentation.

faqs.question: How much does Slite cost
faqs.answer: Slite starts at 10 dollars per user per month for the Basic plan billed annually, with a Pro plan at 20 dollars per user per month, and custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Does Slite have an AI assistant
faqs.answer: Yes, Slite includes an AI assistant called Ask that answers employee questions directly from verified knowledge base content, and a broader Slite Agent on the Pro plan that can also search connected tools.

faqs.question: Is Slite the same company as Slab
faqs.answer: No, Slite and Slab are separate companies in the knowledge base and wiki software category with different founders, funding histories, and product approaches.

faqs.question: Where is Slite headquartered
faqs.answer: Slite is headquartered in Paris, France, and was founded in 2016 by Christophe Pasquier.

faqs.question: Does Slite integrate with other tools
faqs.answer: Yes, on the Pro plan Slite's agent can search across connected tools including Slack, Google Drive, GitHub, Jira, Linear, HubSpot, Salesforce, Intercom, and others.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial of Slite
faqs.answer: Slite offers paid plans starting at the Basic tier; prospective users should check Slite's official pricing page for current trial availability.

faqs.question: Who is Slite best suited for
faqs.answer: Slite is best suited for small to mid-sized teams in engineering, HR, operations, and sales that want a focused, AI-searchable knowledge base rather than a broader all-in-one workspace tool.

use_cases.title: Employee onboarding hub
use_cases.description: New hires can search or ask questions to quickly find onboarding guides, company policies, and team-specific processes without pinging colleagues.

use_cases.title: Engineering documentation
use_cases.description: Engineering teams document architecture decisions, runbooks, and processes in a searchable format that stays current through the verification workflow.

use_cases.title: Cross-tool knowledge retrieval
use_cases.description: Sales and support teams use the Slite Agent to pull answers from connected tools like Salesforce, HubSpot, and Intercom without leaving the knowledge base.

enrichment.short_description: Smallpdf is a Switzerland-based online PDF suite offering compression, conversion, editing, e-signature, and password tools used by over a billion people.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; Pro from $10 per month (billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Smallpdf Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Smallpdf's PDF tools, Pro and Pro for Teams pricing, key features, pros and cons, and FAQs to decide if it fits your document workflow in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Smallpdf is an online PDF toolset founded in 2013 in Zurich, Switzerland, that grew from a single PDF compression tool into a suite of more than 20 tools covering compression, conversion, merging and splitting, editing, e-signature, and password protection or removal. The company is privately held, headquartered in Zurich with an additional team in Belgrade, and employs roughly 50 to 150 people depending on the source consulted. Smallpdf markets itself as having served over 1.7 billion people since launch, positioning it as one of the most widely used browser-based PDF utilities globally, competing with tools like Adobe Acrobat, iLovePDF, and PDFescape. The product runs entirely in the browser or through desktop and mobile apps, letting users upload a document, apply a tool such as compress, convert to Word, or add an e-signature, and download the result without installing dedicated desktop software. Smallpdf uses a freemium pricing model: the Free plan gives access to the core tools with a daily processing or download limit, while paid Pro plans remove those limits and add features like batch processing, more storage, and priority support. A Pro for Teams plan adds shared team management for organizations, and a custom-priced Business plan supports 20 or more users with dedicated support and tailored features. Because it handles sensitive documents, Smallpdf emphasizes encryption and secure file handling, and it integrates with cloud storage providers such as Google Drive and Dropbox for direct file import and export.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Smallpdf
paragraphs: Smallpdf is a browser-based PDF platform founded in Zurich, Switzerland in 2013 that started as a single free tool for compressing PDF files and expanded into a full suite of more than 20 document tools. The company's stated mission is to make PDF and document tasks simple, secure, and reliable, and it claims to have served over 1.7 billion people since launch.

The product covers the most common everyday PDF needs: compressing large files, converting PDFs to and from Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, merging or splitting documents, editing text and images directly in a PDF, adding or removing passwords, and collecting legally binding e-signatures.

Smallpdf is used by individuals, freelancers, and businesses that need quick document processing without installing dedicated desktop software like Adobe Acrobat, and it is accessible through a web browser as well as desktop and mobile apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Smallpdf offers PDF compression, conversion between PDF and Office formats, merging and splitting of multiple files, and an in-browser PDF editor for adding text, images, shapes, and annotations directly onto a document.

The suite also includes e-signature collection for getting documents signed electronically, password protection and removal tools for securing or unlocking PDFs, and an OCR (optical character recognition) tool for making scanned documents searchable and editable.

For teams, Smallpdf offers shared workspace features, integrations with cloud storage services like Google Drive and Dropbox, and centralized billing and user management on its Pro for Teams and Business plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Smallpdf follows a freemium model. The Free plan gives access to the core PDF tools, including compression, conversion, and basic editing, but caps usage with a daily download or processing limit, making it suitable for occasional, light use.

The Pro plan is aimed at individual power users who need unlimited processing, priced at roughly 10 dollars per month when billed annually or 15 dollars per month when billed monthly, and it removes the daily limits present on the free tier while unlocking batch tools and priority support.

Pro for Teams brings per-seat pricing, starting from around 8 dollars per user per month on annual billing, for organizations that want centralized billing and shared team management, while the custom-priced Business plan is built for larger organizations with 20 or more users that need dedicated support and tailored contract terms.

features.title: PDF compression
features.description: Reduces PDF file size while preserving readable quality, useful for emailing or uploading large documents.

features.title: PDF to Office conversion
features.description: Converts PDFs to and from Word, Excel, and PowerPoint formats without losing formatting.

features.title: Merge and split tools
features.description: Combines multiple PDFs into one file or splits a single PDF into separate documents or page ranges.

features.title: In-browser PDF editor
features.description: Lets users add or edit text, images, shapes, and annotations directly inside a PDF without desktop software.

features.title: E-signature collection
features.description: Enables users to sign documents electronically or request signatures from others.

features.title: Password protection and removal
features.description: Adds or removes password security on PDF files to control document access.

features.title: OCR text recognition
features.description: Converts scanned or image-based PDFs into searchable and editable text.

features.title: Cloud storage integrations
features.description: Connects with services like Google Drive and Dropbox for direct file import and export.

pros: Handles nearly every common PDF task in one browser-based suite instead of juggling multiple tools
Free plan is genuinely usable for light, occasional document tasks
Simple, uncluttered interface that requires no training to use
Pro for Teams and Business plans add centralized billing for organizations with many users
cons: Free plan's daily processing or download limits can be restrictive for frequent users
Some advanced document manipulation tasks are handled better by dedicated desktop software like Adobe Acrobat
Pricing across sources varies for Pro and Pro for Teams tiers, so buyers should confirm current rates on the official site
Heavier PDF editing (complex layout changes) is more limited than in full desktop PDF editors

faqs.question: What is Smallpdf used for
faqs.answer: Smallpdf is used for common PDF tasks such as compressing, converting, merging, splitting, editing, password-protecting, and e-signing PDF documents online.

faqs.question: Is Smallpdf free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Smallpdf offers a Free plan with access to core tools, though it includes a daily processing or download limit; paid Pro plans remove that limit.

faqs.question: How much does Smallpdf Pro cost
faqs.answer: Smallpdf Pro is priced around 10 dollars per month when billed annually or 15 dollars per month when billed monthly, based on the official pricing page.

faqs.question: Where is Smallpdf based
faqs.answer: Smallpdf is headquartered in Zurich, Switzerland, and was founded in 2013.

faqs.question: Does Smallpdf offer team plans
faqs.answer: Yes, Smallpdf offers a Pro for Teams plan with per-seat pricing and a custom-priced Business plan for organizations with 20 or more users.

faqs.question: Is Smallpdf secure for sensitive documents
faqs.answer: Smallpdf uses encryption for file handling and processing, though users handling highly sensitive or regulated documents should review its current security and compliance documentation.

faqs.question: Can Smallpdf convert PDFs to Word
faqs.answer: Yes, Smallpdf includes tools to convert PDFs to and from Word, Excel, and PowerPoint formats.

faqs.question: Does Smallpdf have a mobile app
faqs.answer: Yes, Smallpdf offers mobile apps in addition to its browser-based and desktop tools.

use_cases.title: Quick document compression and sharing
use_cases.description: Professionals compress large PDF files before emailing or uploading them to portals with size restrictions.

use_cases.title: Contract signing and processing
use_cases.description: Small businesses use e-signature and password tools to send, sign, and secure contracts and agreements.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and HR teams

use_cases.title: Document format conversion
use_cases.description: Students and administrative staff convert PDFs to editable Word or Excel files for revision and reporting.

enrichment.short_description: Smartlead is a cold email and sales engagement platform focused on deliverability, letting teams send outbound campaigns from unlimited mailboxes with AI-driven warm-up.
enrichment.seo_title: Smartlead Review 2026: Cold Email Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Smartlead's cold email platform works in 2026, including Base, Pro, and Unlimited pricing tiers, deliverability features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Smartlead is a cold email and sales engagement platform founded in 2021 by Vaibhav Namburi, operated by 521 Products Pty Ltd and headquartered in Sydney, Australia. The platform is built around a deliverability-first approach to outbound email, letting sales teams and agencies connect an effectively unlimited number of sending mailboxes to a single account, warm those inboxes up automatically using AI-driven warm-up sequences, and run multi-step outreach campaigns through a unified inbox. Smartlead reports it is used by more than 31,000 businesses, ranging from individual founders to sales agencies managing outbound for multiple clients. Pricing is tiered by usage limits rather than by number of mailboxes, since all plans include unlimited email accounts: the Base plan starts at 39 dollars per month with a cap on contacts stored and emails sent, the Pro plan at 94 dollars per month raises those limits and adds CRM access and API capability, and two Unlimited tiers (Smart and Prime) remove the contact cap entirely and add features like additional dedicated SmartServers, OAuth support, and multiple client workspaces for agencies. Annual billing saves 17 percent across all plans. Beyond the core sending product, Smartlead offers paid add-ons including SmartSenders for managed email provider accounts, SmartDelivery for deliverability monitoring, SmartDialer for voice calling credits, and email verification credits. The platform is aimed primarily at B2B sales teams, growth marketers, and outbound-focused agencies that need to scale cold email volume while keeping messages out of spam folders.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Smartlead
paragraphs: Smartlead is a cold email and sales engagement platform built around the idea that deliverability, not just sending volume, determines the success of an outbound campaign. Founded in 2021 by Vaibhav Namburi and headquartered in Sydney, Australia, the platform lets users connect large numbers of sending mailboxes to a single account and run coordinated outreach campaigns across all of them.

The product is aimed at B2B sales teams, founders doing their own outbound, and agencies running cold email campaigns on behalf of multiple clients. Smartlead reports usage by more than 31,000 businesses, and its feature set reflects agency-style needs such as client workspaces and dedicated sending infrastructure on higher tiers.

Unlike many cold email tools that charge per mailbox, Smartlead includes unlimited email accounts on every plan and instead prices based on the volume of contacts stored and emails sent, which changes the cost calculus for teams running high-volume outbound.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Smartlead's core sending engine supports unlimited mailboxes per account, multi-step email sequences, and a unified master inbox where replies from all connected mailboxes and campaigns can be managed in one place.

AI-powered warm-up gradually builds sender reputation for new or underused inboxes to improve inbox placement, and on higher tiers this becomes Ultra Premium Warmup for more aggressive reputation management. The platform also includes built-in email verification to reduce bounce rates before sending.

For agencies and larger teams, Smartlead offers multiple client workspaces to separate campaigns and reporting by client, CRM access on the Pro tier and above, API and webhook access for custom integrations, and add-on products like SmartDialer for voice calling and SmartDelivery for deliverability monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Smartlead offers four core plans billed monthly, with a 17 percent discount available on annual billing. The Base plan, at 39 dollars per month, includes 2,000 contacts storage, 6,000 email sends, and 2,000 verified prospect emails, making it suited to individuals or small teams starting outbound.

The Pro plan, at 94 dollars per month, raises the limits to 30,000 contacts, 90,000 email sends, and 30,000 verified prospect emails, and adds CRM access. The Unlimited Smart plan, at 174 dollars per month and marked as most popular, removes the contact cap and allows 150,000 email sends with eligibility for Ultra Premium Warmup.

The top-tier Unlimited Prime plan, at 379 dollars per month, supports 500,000 email sends, includes three dedicated SmartServers with OAuth, and supports three or more client workspaces, making it the plan most suited to agencies running outbound at scale. Add-ons such as SmartSenders, SmartDelivery, SmartDialer, and dedicated SmartServers are priced separately.

features.title: Unlimited sending mailboxes
features.description: Every plan allows connecting an unlimited number of email accounts to run campaigns from, rather than charging per mailbox.

features.description: Consolidates replies from all connected mailboxes and campaigns into a single inbox for reps to manage.

features.title: Multi-step campaign sequences
features.description: Supports automated, multi-touch email sequences with conditional logic and follow-ups.

features.title: Built-in email verification
features.description: Verifies prospect email addresses before sending to reduce bounce rates and protect sender reputation.

features.title: Client workspaces for agencies
features.description: Higher-tier plans support multiple isolated client workspaces, useful for agencies managing outbound for several clients.

features.title: CRM and API access
features.description: Pro and higher plans include native CRM access along with API and webhook access for custom integrations.

features.title: SmartDialer add-on
features.description: An optional voice calling add-on that lets teams combine cold calling with email outreach from the same platform.

pros: All plans include unlimited sending mailboxes, which can be more cost-effective than per-mailbox pricing tools
Strong deliverability focus with AI warm-up and dedicated SmartServers on higher tiers
Agency-friendly features like multiple client workspaces on the Unlimited Prime plan
Annual billing offers a meaningful 17 percent discount across all tiers
cons: Pricing is based on contact and send volume, which can make costs harder to predict at scale than flat per-seat pricing
Several capabilities like CRM access are gated behind the Pro plan and above
Add-ons such as SmartServers and SmartDelivery add extra cost on top of the base subscription
Relatively young company (founded 2021) compared to some longer-established sales engagement platforms

faqs.question: What is Smartlead used for
faqs.answer: Smartlead is used for cold email outreach and sales engagement, letting teams send campaigns from unlimited mailboxes while managing deliverability and replies in one platform.

faqs.question: How much does Smartlead cost
faqs.answer: Smartlead's plans start at 39 dollars per month for the Base plan and go up to 379 dollars per month for the Unlimited Prime plan, with a 17 percent discount for annual billing.

faqs.question: Does Smartlead limit the number of mailboxes
faqs.answer: No, all Smartlead plans include unlimited email accounts; pricing is instead based on contacts stored and emails sent.

faqs.question: Is Smartlead good for agencies
faqs.answer: Yes, higher-tier plans include multiple client workspaces and dedicated sending infrastructure that are commonly used by agencies managing outbound for several clients.

faqs.question: Where is Smartlead based
faqs.answer: Smartlead is headquartered in Sydney, Australia, and was founded in 2021 by Vaibhav Namburi.

faqs.question: Does Smartlead include email warm-up
faqs.answer: Yes, Smartlead includes AI-powered warm-up to build sender reputation, with an Ultra Premium Warmup option available on Unlimited plans.

faqs.question: Does Smartlead have a CRM
faqs.answer: Smartlead includes CRM access starting on its Pro plan and above.

faqs.question: Can Smartlead verify email addresses
faqs.answer: Yes, Smartlead includes built-in email verification credits to reduce bounce rates before sending campaigns.

use_cases.title: High-volume cold outbound

use_cases.title: Agency client outreach management

enrichment.short_description: SmartRecruiters is a talent acquisition suite for sourcing, screening, and hiring at scale, now operating as part of SAP following its 2025 acquisition.
enrichment.pricing_model: custom quote (enterprise, priced by employee headcount)
enrichment.seo_title: SmartRecruiters Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how SmartRecruiters' talent acquisition suite works in 2026, including Essential and Enterprise pricing, key features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SmartRecruiters is a talent acquisition and recruiting software platform founded in 2010 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Before being acquired, the company employed roughly 700 to 750 people and had raised significant venture funding to build out its applicant tracking system and broader hiring suite, which includes sourcing, candidate relationship management, interview scheduling, offer management, and reporting tools used by large and mid-sized employers. In September 2025, SAP completed its acquisition of SmartRecruiters, folding the platform into SAP's human capital management portfolio; industry sources expect a revised licensing and pricing structure to roll out in 2026 as the product is integrated with SAP SuccessFactors and related offerings. SmartRecruiters prices its platform based on a company's total employee headcount rather than the number of recruiter seats, with the Essential tier publicly listed at 14,995 dollars per year and higher Professional, High Volume, and Complete tiers requiring a custom quote that can range from roughly 30,000 to well over 120,000 dollars annually depending on organization size and feature needs. Implementation typically adds 5,000 to 25,000 dollars, and custom integrations are priced separately. The platform is aimed at mid-market and enterprise talent acquisition teams that need a modern, configurable applicant tracking system with strong sourcing and candidate experience tools, and it has historically been used by well-known global employers managing high volumes of hiring across multiple countries and business units.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SmartRecruiters
paragraphs: SmartRecruiters is a talent acquisition suite built around a modern applicant tracking system, designed to help mid-market and enterprise organizations manage the full hiring lifecycle from sourcing candidates through making an offer. Founded in 2010 and headquartered in San Francisco, the company built its reputation on a more open, marketplace-style platform that integrates with a wide range of job boards, assessment providers, and background check vendors.

In September 2025, SAP completed its acquisition of SmartRecruiters, bringing the platform into SAP's human capital management lineup alongside SuccessFactors. This is a significant change for prospective and existing customers, as licensing, pricing, and product roadmap decisions are expected to evolve as integration with SAP's broader enterprise software ecosystem continues through 2026.

SmartRecruiters is generally positioned for organizations with more complex, high-volume, or global hiring needs, rather than very small businesses, given its enterprise-oriented pricing model and depth of configuration options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of SmartRecruiters is its applicant tracking system, which manages job requisitions, candidate pipelines, interview scheduling, collaborative hiring team feedback, and offer management in one place, with configurable workflows for different departments or regions.

Sourcing tools include a marketplace of integrated job boards and vendors, career site and job posting management, and candidate relationship management features for building and nurturing talent pipelines ahead of open positions.

The platform also provides reporting and analytics on hiring funnel performance, diversity and inclusion metrics, and time-to-hire, along with mobile apps for hiring managers and an API for connecting to HRIS and other enterprise systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: SmartRecruiters prices its platform based on a company's total employee headcount rather than the number of recruiters using the system, which is a departure from many competing applicant tracking systems that charge per seat.
The Essential plan is the only tier with published list pricing, starting at 14,995 dollars per year, and is intended for smaller organizations with more limited hiring volume and feature needs.

The Professional, High Volume, and Complete tiers all require a custom quote from SmartRecruiters' sales team, with typical enterprise contracts reported to range from roughly 30,000 to over 120,000 dollars annually depending on headcount and feature requirements. Implementation fees of 5,000 to 25,000 dollars and separately priced custom integrations should be factored into total cost of ownership, and buyers should confirm current terms directly given the ongoing transition following the SAP acquisition.

features.title: Configurable applicant tracking system
features.description: Manages job requisitions, candidate pipelines, and hiring workflows with configuration options for different teams and regions.

features.title: Sourcing marketplace
features.description: Integrates with a wide range of job boards, sourcing vendors, and assessment providers to help fill pipelines.

features.title: Candidate relationship management
features.description: Helps recruiters build and nurture talent pools ahead of open roles rather than starting from zero each time.

features.title: Collaborative interview management
features.description: Coordinates interview scheduling and structured hiring team feedback within the platform.

features.title: Offer management
features.description: Supports creating, approving, and sending offers as part of the end-to-end hiring workflow.

features.title: Hiring analytics and reporting
features.description: Provides visibility into funnel performance, time-to-hire, and diversity and inclusion metrics.

features.title: Mobile hiring manager app
features.description: Lets hiring managers review candidates and provide feedback from a mobile device.

features.title: API and HRIS integrations
features.description: Connects SmartRecruiters to broader HR systems, increasingly including SAP SuccessFactors following the 2025 acquisition.

pros: Deep configurability suited to complex, high-volume, or global hiring workflows
Large marketplace of job board and vendor integrations reduces the need for separate sourcing tools
Strong analytics and reporting for tracking hiring funnel performance
Now backed by SAP's resources following the 2025 acquisition, which may bring deeper enterprise integrations
cons: Pricing beyond the Essential tier is not published and requires a custom quote, making budgeting harder upfront
Enterprise-oriented pricing and implementation costs make it a poor fit for very small businesses
Ongoing integration with SAP following the 2025 acquisition creates some roadmap and pricing uncertainty for 2026
Implementation and custom integration fees can add significantly to the first-year cost

faqs.question: What is SmartRecruiters used for
faqs.answer: SmartRecruiters is used as an applicant tracking system and talent acquisition suite for sourcing, screening, interviewing, and hiring candidates.

faqs.question: How much does SmartRecruiters cost
faqs.answer: The Essential plan is listed at 14,995 dollars per year; higher Professional, High Volume, and Complete tiers require a custom quote typically ranging from about 30,000 to over 120,000 dollars annually.

faqs.question: Is SmartRecruiters still an independent company
faqs.answer: No, SAP completed its acquisition of SmartRecruiters in September 2025, and the platform now operates as part of SAP's human capital management portfolio.

faqs.question: Where is SmartRecruiters headquartered
faqs.answer: SmartRecruiters was founded in 2010 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: How is SmartRecruiters priced
faqs.answer: SmartRecruiters prices its plans based on a company's total employee headcount rather than the number of recruiter seats using the system.

faqs.question: Does SmartRecruiters integrate with SAP SuccessFactors
faqs.answer: Following the September 2025 acquisition, deeper integration between SmartRecruiters and SAP SuccessFactors is expected to develop through 2026.

faqs.question: Is SmartRecruiters suited for small businesses
faqs.answer: Not typically; its enterprise-oriented pricing and implementation costs make it better suited to mid-market and large organizations with complex hiring needs.

faqs.question: Does SmartRecruiters offer a mobile app
faqs.answer: Yes, SmartRecruiters provides a mobile app for hiring managers to review candidates and give feedback on the go.

use_cases.title: High-volume enterprise hiring
use_cases.description: Large employers use SmartRecruiters to manage thousands of open requisitions and candidates across departments and regions with configurable workflows.

use_cases.title: Global recruiting operations
use_cases.description: Multinational organizations use SmartRecruiters' marketplace of vendors and integrations to standardize hiring across different countries.

use_cases.title: Employer branding and candidate pipelines
use_cases.description: Recruiting teams use candidate relationship management tools to build talent pools ahead of anticipated hiring needs.

enrichment.short_description: Smartsheet is a spreadsheet-style work management and project collaboration platform used by the majority of Fortune 500 companies, now privately held after a 2025 buyout.
enrichment.starting_price: $9 per user per month (Pro plan, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Smartsheet Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Smartsheet's work management platform works in 2026, including Pro and Business pricing, key features, pros, cons, and frequently asked questions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Smartsheet is a work management platform founded in 2005 by W. Eric Browne, Maria Colacurcio, John D. Creason, and Brent R. Frei, and headquartered in Bellevue, Washington. The company built its product around a familiar spreadsheet-style grid interface layered with project management features such as Gantt charts, dashboards, automated workflows, and forms, which let it reach a very wide range of non-technical business users in addition to project managers. Smartsheet went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker SMAR in April 2018, and it grew to be used by roughly 85 percent of Fortune 500 companies according to the company's own marketing. In September 2024, Vista Equity Partners and Blackstone announced an agreement to acquire Smartsheet for approximately 8.4 billion dollars in cash at 56.50 dollars per share, and that acquisition closed in January 2025, meaning Smartsheet is now a privately held company rather than a publicly traded one, even though its NYSE: SMAR ticker remains part of its recent history. The platform offers tiered per-user pricing: a Pro plan at 9 dollars per user per month (billed annually) aimed at smaller teams with a cap on licensed users, and a Business plan at 32 dollars per user per month that removes the user cap and adds features like WorkApps, Data Shuttle, and expanded automation. An Enterprise tier with custom pricing adds advanced security, governance, and integration capabilities for large organizations. Smartsheet is commonly used for project tracking, resource management, marketing campaign planning, and IT operations across large, complex organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Smartsheet
paragraphs: Smartsheet is a work management platform that combines a familiar spreadsheet-style grid with project management capabilities such as Gantt charts, dashboards, forms, and workflow automation. Founded in 2005 and headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, the company built its product to be approachable for business users who are comfortable with spreadsheets but need more structure and collaboration than a plain spreadsheet file can offer.

The company went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker SMAR in April 2018 and grew into one of the more widely adopted enterprise work management platforms, with the company reporting usage by roughly 85 percent of Fortune 500 companies. In September 2024, Vista Equity Partners and Blackstone agreed to acquire Smartsheet for about 8.4 billion dollars, and that deal closed in January 2025, taking the company private again.

Smartsheet is used across a wide range of departments and industries for project tracking, resource and capacity planning, marketing campaign management, and IT operations, largely because its grid-based interface lowers the learning curve compared to more specialized project management tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Smartsheet's core building block is the sheet, a spreadsheet-like grid that can be enhanced with row hierarchies, dependencies, and automatically generated Gantt charts and calendar views, letting teams plan and track work without learning an entirely new interface.

Dashboards and reports let teams aggregate data across multiple sheets into real-time visual summaries for stakeholders, while Smartsheet's automation engine (workflows) can trigger notifications, approval requests, and status updates based on conditions set on the data.

On higher tiers, Smartsheet adds WorkApps for building lightweight custom applications on top of sheet data, Data Shuttle for automating data imports and exports, and forms for structured data collection that feeds directly into a sheet.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Smartsheet's Pro plan is priced at 9 dollars per user per month when billed annually (versus 12 dollars per month billed monthly), and includes 20 GB of attachment storage along with core sheets, dashboards, forms, and automation, though it is capped at a limited number of licensed users.

The Business plan, at 32 dollars per user per month, removes the user cap present on Pro and adds unlimited attachment storage, WorkApps for building custom lightweight apps, Data Shuttle for automated data movement, and a higher monthly automation allowance, making it the more common choice for larger or cross-departmental deployments.

An Enterprise plan with custom pricing is available for organizations that need advanced security controls, single sign-on, governance features, and deeper integrations, typically negotiated directly with Smartsheet's sales team based on organization size and requirements.

features.title: Spreadsheet-style grid interface
features.description: Combines familiar spreadsheet rows and columns with row hierarchies and dependencies for project-style planning.

features.title: Automatic Gantt and calendar views
features.description: Generates Gantt charts and calendar views directly from sheet data without manual chart building.

features.title: Real-time dashboards and reports
features.description: Aggregates data from multiple sheets into visual dashboards for stakeholders and leadership.

features.title: Workflow automation
features.description: Triggers notifications, approvals, and status changes automatically based on conditions in the data.

features.title: WorkApps
features.description: Lets teams build lightweight custom applications on top of sheet data without traditional coding, available on Business and above.

features.title: Forms for data collection
features.description: Structured forms feed submitted data directly into sheets for tracking requests, intake, or approvals.

features.title: Data Shuttle
features.description: Automates recurring data imports and exports between Smartsheet and other systems, available on Business and above.

features.title: Broad third-party integrations
features.description: Connects with tools like Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Slack, and Salesforce for cross-platform workflows.

pros: Low learning curve for teams already comfortable with spreadsheets
Used at scale by a large share of Fortune 500 companies, indicating strong enterprise reliability
Flexible enough to support project management, resource planning, and lightweight app-building on one platform
Strong automation and reporting capabilities reduce manual status updates
cons: Business plan pricing at 32 dollars per user per month can get expensive for large teams compared to some competitors
Pro plan's user cap limits its usefulness for larger deployments without upgrading
Spreadsheet-based interface can feel limiting for teams that need more visual, board-based project views without add-ons
Now privately held after the 2025 Vista Equity Partners and Blackstone acquisition, which may affect long-term public roadmap transparency

faqs.question: What is Smartsheet used for
faqs.answer: Smartsheet is used for project management, resource planning, marketing campaign tracking, and general work management using a spreadsheet-style interface.

faqs.question: How much does Smartsheet cost
faqs.answer: Smartsheet's Pro plan starts at 9 dollars per user per month billed annually, and the Business plan is 32 dollars per user per month, with custom Enterprise pricing available.

faqs.question: Is Smartsheet still publicly traded
faqs.answer: No, Smartsheet was publicly traded on the NYSE under the ticker SMAR until Vista Equity Partners and Blackstone completed a buyout in January 2025, making it privately held again.

faqs.question: Where is Smartsheet headquartered
faqs.answer: Smartsheet is headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, and was founded in 2005.

faqs.question: Does Smartsheet have Gantt charts
faqs.answer: Yes, Smartsheet automatically generates Gantt chart and calendar views from data entered into its spreadsheet-style sheets.

faqs.question: Can Smartsheet build custom apps
faqs.answer: Yes, the WorkApps feature, available on the Business plan and above, lets teams build lightweight custom applications on top of sheet data.

faqs.question: Is Smartsheet used by large enterprises
faqs.answer: Yes, Smartsheet reports that roughly 85 percent of Fortune 500 companies use its platform.

faqs.question: Does Smartsheet integrate with Microsoft and Google tools
faqs.answer: Yes, Smartsheet integrates with Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Slack, Salesforce, and other common business tools.

use_cases.title: Enterprise project and portfolio tracking
use_cases.description: Project management offices use Smartsheet to track multiple projects, dependencies, and resource allocation across departments.

use_cases.title: Marketing campaign management
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use sheets, forms, and dashboards to plan campaigns, collect creative requests, and report on status to leadership.

use_cases.title: IT and operations workflow automation
use_cases.description: IT teams build lightweight WorkApps and automated workflows on top of Smartsheet data to manage tickets, approvals, and asset tracking.

enrichment.short_description: Smartsupp is a Czech live chat, chatbot, and AI customer support platform that helps online stores and websites engage visitors and increase sales.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans from $17 per month (billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Smartsupp Review 2026: Live Chat Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Smartsupp's live chat and AI chatbot platform in 2026, including Free, Solo, Plus, and Expert pricing, key features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Smartsupp is a live chat, chatbot, and AI customer support platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in Brno, Czech Republic, with an estimated 11 to 50 employees. The company built its product to help e-commerce stores and general business websites engage visitors in real time, combining live chat, an AI chatbot agent branded Mira, and integrations with WhatsApp and Facebook Messenger so support teams can manage conversations across channels from one inbox. Smartsupp positions itself as a tool for increasing online sales, not just handling support tickets, with features like visitor tracking and proactive chat triggers designed to engage shoppers before they abandon a purchase. Pricing follows a freemium structure: the Free plan supports one operator and 25 conversations per month with 14-day conversation history, while paid tiers unlocking unlimited conversations start with the Solo plan at roughly 17 dollars per month for one operator, moving up through Plus and Expert plans that add more operators, more email channels, automation workflows, and premium support, with an Enterprise tier offering custom limits and a dedicated account manager. Smartsupp separately sells its Mira AI Agent as an add-on starting around 16 dollars per month for a set number of AI-handled conversations, which can be scaled up or replaced with an unlimited AI agent plan on custom pricing. The platform is aimed primarily at small and mid-sized online retailers and service businesses, particularly in Europe, that want an affordable way to combine human live chat with AI-assisted automation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Smartsupp
paragraphs: Smartsupp is a live chat and AI-assisted customer support platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in Brno, Czech Republic. The company built its product around the idea that real-time engagement with website visitors can directly increase online sales, not just resolve support questions after the fact.

The platform combines traditional live chat with an AI chatbot, branded Mira, and multichannel messaging support for WhatsApp and Facebook Messenger, letting small and mid-sized businesses manage conversations from multiple channels in a single unified inbox.

Smartsupp is particularly popular among European e-commerce businesses and service providers that want an affordable alternative to larger enterprise live chat platforms, while still offering AI automation for handling routine questions without adding headcount.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of Smartsupp is real-time live chat, which lets support agents respond to website visitors instantly, view visitor activity and browsing behavior, and trigger proactive chat messages based on visitor behavior to reduce cart abandonment.

Smartsupp's Mira AI Agent can handle a set volume of conversations automatically, answering common questions from a knowledge base of up to tens of thousands of products and dozens of content pages, and can hand off complex conversations to a human agent seamlessly when needed.

The platform also supports multichannel messaging through WhatsApp and Facebook Messenger integrations, automation workflows for routing and tagging conversations, and mobile apps so support teams can respond to visitors from anywhere.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Smartsupp's Free plan supports one operator and up to 25 conversations per month with 14 days of conversation history, making it usable for very small sites but limited for active support volume.

Paid plans scale from Solo (about 17 dollars per month for one operator with unlimited conversations) through Plus (about 25 dollars per month for three operators and additional email channels) to Expert (about 83 dollars per month for five or more operators, premium support, and team features), all billed annually with roughly 30 percent savings versus monthly billing. An Enterprise tier starting around 280 dollars per month offers custom limits, a dedicated account manager, and API access.

Smartsupp's Mira AI Agent is sold as a separate add-on starting around 16 dollars per month for a limited number of AI-handled conversations per month, with the option to scale up conversation volume or move to a custom-priced unlimited AI agent plan for larger operations.

features.description: Automatically engages visitors based on behavior, such as time on page or cart activity, to reduce abandonment.

features.title: Automation workflows
features.description: Routes and tags conversations automatically based on rules to speed up response times.

features.title: Visitor tracking
features.description: Shows agents real-time visitor browsing data to give context before or during a conversation.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Lets support teams respond to live chat conversations from mobile devices.

features.title: Knowledge base integration for AI
features.description: Mira can be trained on a store's product catalog and content pages to answer product-specific questions automatically.

pros: Genuinely usable free plan for very small sites or early-stage businesses
Combines live chat and AI chatbot in one affordable platform rather than requiring separate tools
Multichannel support for WhatsApp and Messenger in addition to on-site chat
Annual billing offers a meaningful roughly 30 percent discount over monthly pricing
cons: Free plan's 25 conversation per month cap is quite limiting for active stores
Mira AI Agent is a separate add-on with its own pricing rather than being bundled into core plans
Higher operator counts on Expert and Enterprise plans can add up in cost for larger support teams
Smaller company size relative to larger live chat competitors like Intercom or Zendesk

faqs.question: What is Smartsupp used for
faqs.answer: Smartsupp is used for live chat, AI chatbot support, and multichannel messaging to help websites and online stores engage visitors and increase sales.

faqs.answer: Yes, Smartsupp offers a Free plan for one operator with up to 25 conversations per month; paid plans remove that conversation limit.

faqs.question: How much does Smartsupp cost
faqs.answer: Paid Smartsupp plans start around 17 dollars per month for the Solo plan, scaling up to around 83 dollars per month for the Expert plan, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Where is Smartsupp based

faqs.question: Does Smartsupp support WhatsApp and Messenger
faqs.answer: Yes, Smartsupp integrates with WhatsApp and Facebook Messenger so all conversations can be managed alongside live chat in one inbox.

faqs.question: Is Smartsupp good for e-commerce
faqs.answer: Yes, Smartsupp is commonly used by online stores for proactive engagement, visitor tracking, and reducing cart abandonment through live chat.

faqs.question: Does Smartsupp charge extra for AI features
faqs.answer: Yes, the Mira AI Agent is sold as a separate add-on plan starting around 16 dollars per month, in addition to core live chat plans.

enrichment.short_description: Smokeball is a cloud-based legal practice management platform that combines document automation, time tracking, billing, and case management for small and mid-sized law firms.
enrichment.starting_price: From $149 per month (varies by firm size and 12 or 36-month term)
enrichment.headquarters: Chicago, Illinois (global headquarters in Sydney, Australia)
enrichment.seo_title: Smokeball Review 2026: Legal Software Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Smokeball's legal practice management software works in 2026, including Bill, Boost, Grow, and Prosper+ plans, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Smokeball is a cloud-based legal practice management platform founded in 2010 by Hunter Steele, Jane Oxley, and Bart Vadala, with a global head office in Sydney, Australia and a US headquarters in Chicago, Illinois established after the company expanded into the United States in 2013. Smokeball raised a 30 million dollar Series B funding round in September 2021 and has grown into one of the more widely used practice management platforms for small US law firms across practice areas including family law, personal injury, real estate, estate planning, litigation, and criminal law. The platform combines document management and automation, time and expense tracking, trust accounting, billing and online payments, client and matter management, calendaring, and an AI assistant called Archie that helps with drafting and client communication. Smokeball's pricing is organized into four named tiers, Bill, Boost, Grow, and Prosper+, each of increasing feature depth, all listed on the company's pricing page as starting from 149 dollars per month, with actual per-firm pricing varying based on the number of users (1-2, 3-9, or 10-plus) and whether the firm commits to a 12-month or 36-month contract term; firms must request a personalized quote to see exact figures. Add-ons available on top of the core plans include the Archie AI assistant, AutoTime for automatic time capture, Intake for lead and client intake management, FamilyPro for family law-specific tools, and Workflows for advanced process automation. Smokeball is positioned specifically for small to mid-sized law firms in the United States and Australia that want an integrated, cloud-based alternative to juggling separate billing, document, and case management tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Smokeball
paragraphs: Smokeball is a cloud-based legal practice management platform founded in 2010 by a team of Australian legal technology veterans, Hunter Steele, Jane Oxley, and Bart Vadala. The company maintains a global head office in Sydney, Australia, and expanded into the United States in 2013 with a Chicago office that now serves as its US headquarters.

The platform is built specifically for small and mid-sized law firms and combines the core operational tools a firm needs day to day: document management and automation, time and expense tracking, trust accounting, billing, client and matter management, and calendaring, all inside one cloud-based system rather than several disconnected tools.

Smokeball has raised institutional funding, including a 30 million dollar Series B round in September 2021, and is used across a range of practice areas including family law, personal injury, real estate, estate planning, litigation, business sale and purchase, and criminal law.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Smokeball's document automation engine generates legal documents from firm-specific and practice area templates, reducing repetitive drafting work, and its Microsoft Word and Outlook integrations let attorneys work inside familiar tools while Smokeball automatically captures time and files documents to the right matter in the background.

Time and expense tracking, trust accounting, and billing are handled natively, with online payment collection and accounting integrations to reduce manual reconciliation, and a client portal allows secure document sharing and communication with clients.

Higher tiers add an AI assistant called Archie for drafting assistance and client communication, lead and intake management tools, firm-wide insights and profitability reporting, and advanced custom workflows and templates for firms with more complex process needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Smokeball organizes its pricing into four named plans of increasing capability: Bill, which covers invoicing, time and expense tracking, online payments, and trust accounting; Boost, which adds full client and matter management, document management, calendaring, and a client portal; Grow, which layers on practice area templates, advanced document automation, and email management; and Prosper+, the top tier, which adds firm insights reporting, lead management, and advanced matter intake.

On its official pricing page, Smokeball lists all four plans as starting from 149 dollars per month, with the exact price depending on firm size (1-2 users, 3-9 users, or 10 or more users) and whether the firm signs a 12-month or 36-month contract, longer terms generally reducing the effective monthly rate.

Because Smokeball does not publish granular per-tier, per-user pricing beyond the starting figure, firms need to request a personalized quote through the company's sales team to get an exact price, and can add optional modules like the Archie AI assistant, AutoTime, Intake, FamilyPro, and Workflows on top of their base plan.

features.title: Document automation
features.description: Generates legal documents from firm and practice area templates to reduce repetitive manual drafting.

features.title: Automatic time capture
features.description: Tracks attorney time in the background as they work in Microsoft Word and Outlook, reducing missed billable time.

features.title: Trust accounting and billing
features.description: Manages trust accounts, invoicing, and online payment collection natively within the platform.

features.title: Matter and client management
features.description: Centralizes client and case information, communications, and documents in one place per matter.

features.title: Archie AI assistant
features.description: An AI-powered assistant that helps with drafting and client communication, available as an add-on on higher plans.

features.title: Client portal
features.description: Provides secure document sharing and communication between the firm and its clients.

features.title: Legal calendaring
features.description: Manages court dates, deadlines, and appointments integrated with matter records.

features.title: Firm insights and profitability reporting
features.description: Gives firm leadership visibility into staff activity, matter profitability, and overall performance, available on top tiers.

pros: Combines document management, billing, trust accounting, and case management in one integrated platform
Automatic time capture in Word and Outlook can meaningfully reduce lost billable hours
Built specifically for small law firm workflows across common practice areas
Flexible add-ons like Archie AI and FamilyPro let firms customize the platform to their practice
cons: Public pricing page only shows a starting figure, requiring a sales call to get an exact quote
Longer 36-month contract terms may not suit firms wanting more pricing flexibility
Higher-tier features like advanced automation and AI assistance require moving up to more expensive plans
Primarily built for US and Australian law firms, which may limit fit for firms in other jurisdictions

faqs.question: What is Smokeball used for
faqs.answer: Smokeball is used as legal practice management software for small and mid-sized law firms, combining document management, time tracking, billing, and case management.

faqs.question: How much does Smokeball cost
faqs.answer: Smokeball's plans are listed as starting from 149 dollars per month, with exact pricing depending on firm size and whether a firm commits to a 12-month or 36-month contract.

faqs.question: Where is Smokeball based
faqs.answer: Smokeball has a global head office in Sydney, Australia, and a US headquarters in Chicago, Illinois, and was founded in 2010.

faqs.question: Does Smokeball include trust accounting
faqs.answer: Yes, trust accounting and reporting are included starting with the Bill plan.

faqs.question: Does Smokeball have an AI assistant
faqs.answer: Yes, Smokeball offers an AI assistant called Archie for drafting and client communication as an add-on module.

faqs.question: What practice areas does Smokeball support
faqs.answer: Smokeball is used across practice areas including family law, personal injury, real estate, estate planning, litigation, and criminal law.

faqs.question: Does Smokeball automatically track billable time
faqs.answer: Yes, Smokeball automatically captures time as attorneys work in Microsoft Word and Outlook, reducing manually logged hours.

faqs.question: Is Smokeball cloud-based
faqs.answer: Yes, Smokeball is a cloud-based platform, and its Boost plan and above are fully browser-accessible.

use_cases.description: Firm leadership uses Prosper+ reporting to track staff activity, matter profitability, and lead conversion as the firm scales.

enrichment.short_description: Snappa is a Canadian web-based graphic design tool that lets small businesses and social media managers create marketing images using templates, stock photos, and a drag-and-drop editor.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; Pro from $10 per month (billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Snappa Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Snappa's graphic design tool works in 2026, including Starter, Pro, and Team pricing, key features, pros, cons, and how it compares to Canva.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Snappa is a web-based graphic design platform founded in 2015 in Ottawa, Canada by Marc Chouinard and Christopher Gimmer, who serve as the company's CTO and CEO respectively. The company is a small, largely bootstrapped team, reported to run with roughly 2 to 10 employees while generating an estimated 3.2 million dollars in revenue in 2024, up from 2.3 million dollars the prior year. Snappa is designed for people without design experience, primarily small business owners, marketers, and social media managers, who need to create graphics for platforms like Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, YouTube, and blog headers without learning complex software like Photoshop. The drag-and-drop editor includes access to more than 6,000 templates and over 5 million royalty-free HD photos and graphics, along with one-click tools for background removal and resizing designs across multiple social media dimensions. Pricing follows a freemium model: the Starter plan is free for one user with access to all templates and photos but limited to 3 downloads per month, while the Pro plan (10 dollars per month billed annually, or 15 dollars per month billed monthly) removes the download cap for a single user and adds custom font uploads, background removal, and social media scheduling integrations such as Buffer. The Team plan, at 20 dollars per month annually or 30 dollars per month monthly, extends the same feature set to five users with team collaboration tools, and larger teams needing more than five seats must contact Snappa directly for custom pricing. Snappa is frequently compared to Canva as a simpler, more affordable alternative focused specifically on social media and marketing graphics rather than broader design or presentation use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Snappa
paragraphs: Snappa is a web-based graphic design tool founded in 2015 in Ottawa, Canada by Marc Chouinard and Christopher Gimmer, built specifically to let people without design training create professional-looking marketing graphics quickly. The company runs as a small, largely bootstrapped team and has grown steadily, reporting 3.2 million dollars in revenue in 2024.

The platform is aimed squarely at small business owners, marketers, and social media managers who need graphics for platforms like Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube, as well as blog headers and ads, without learning more complex tools like Photoshop or Illustrator.
Snappa is commonly compared to Canva, and is generally positioned as a simpler, more affordable, and more social-media-focused alternative rather than a broad design and presentation platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Snappa's core editor is a drag-and-drop design canvas with more than 6,000 templates pre-sized for social media posts, ads, blog graphics, and other common marketing formats, so users do not need to calculate dimensions manually.

The platform includes access to over 5 million royalty-free HD stock photos and graphics directly inside the editor, along with one-click background removal and the ability to instantly resize a single design into multiple social media dimensions without rebuilding it from scratch.

Paid plans add custom font uploads for on-brand designs, direct integrations with social scheduling tools like Buffer for publishing straight from the editor, and, on the Team plan, collaboration features that let multiple users share and work within the same account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Snappa's Starter plan is free for one user and includes access to all 6,000-plus templates and the full stock photo and graphics library, but limits users to 3 downloads per month, making it suitable mainly for trying out the platform or very light use.

The Pro plan costs 10 dollars per month when billed annually or 15 dollars per month when billed monthly, and removes the download cap for a single user while adding unlimited downloads, custom font uploads, background removal, and social media scheduling integrations.

The Team plan costs 20 dollars per month billed annually or 30 dollars per month billed monthly, and extends the same unlimited-download feature set to five users with team collaboration tools; businesses needing more than five seats must contact Snappa directly for custom pricing.

features.title: Drag-and-drop design editor
features.description: An easy-to-use canvas for building social media graphics, ads, and blog headers without design experience.

features.title: 6,000-plus templates
features.description: Pre-sized templates for major social platforms and common marketing graphic formats.

features.title: 5 million-plus stock photos and graphics
features.description: A built-in royalty-free photo and graphics library accessible directly inside the editor.

features.title: One-click background removal
features.description: Automatically removes image backgrounds without needing separate photo editing software.

features.title: Instant multi-format resizing
features.description: Resizes a single design across multiple social media dimensions without rebuilding it.

features.title: Custom font uploads
features.description: Lets paid users upload brand-specific fonts for on-brand design consistency.

features.title: Social scheduling integrations
features.description: Connects with tools like Buffer to publish designs directly to social media from within Snappa.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: The Team plan supports up to five users working within a shared account and design library.

pros: Simple, focused interface that is easy to learn for non-designers
Generous free plan includes full access to templates and stock photos, just with a download cap
More affordable than many competitors for small teams needing basic social media graphics
One-click background removal and multi-format resizing save significant manual editing time
cons: Feature set is narrower than broader design platforms like Canva, with less support for presentations or complex layouts
Team plan caps out at five users, requiring a custom quote for larger teams
Free plan's 3 download per month limit is restrictive for anyone posting regularly
Small company size (roughly 2-10 employees) may mean slower feature development compared to larger competitors

faqs.question: What is Snappa used for
faqs.answer: Snappa is used to create social media graphics, ads, blog headers, and other marketing images using templates, stock photos, and a drag-and-drop editor.

faqs.question: Is Snappa free
faqs.answer: Yes, Snappa offers a free Starter plan with access to all templates and stock photos, limited to 3 downloads per month.

faqs.question: How much does Snappa Pro cost
faqs.answer: Snappa Pro costs 10 dollars per month when billed annually or 15 dollars per month when billed monthly, and removes the download limit for one user.

faqs.question: Where is Snappa based
faqs.answer: Snappa is based in Ottawa, Canada, and was founded in 2015 by Marc Chouinard and Christopher Gimmer.

faqs.question: How is Snappa different from Canva
faqs.answer: Snappa is generally positioned as a simpler, more affordable alternative focused specifically on social media and marketing graphics, while Canva covers a broader range of design and presentation use cases.

faqs.question: Does Snappa support team collaboration
faqs.answer: Yes, the Team plan supports up to five users collaborating within a shared account; larger teams need to contact Snappa for custom pricing.

faqs.question: Does Snappa include stock photos
faqs.answer: Yes, Snappa includes access to more than 5 million royalty-free HD stock photos and graphics directly in the editor.

faqs.question: Can Snappa remove image backgrounds
faqs.answer: Yes, Snappa includes a one-click background removal tool on its paid plans.

use_cases.title: Social media content creation
use_cases.description: Social media managers use Snappa's templates and instant resizing to produce consistent posts across multiple platforms quickly.

use_cases.title: Small business marketing graphics
use_cases.description: Small business owners create ads, promotional graphics, and blog headers without hiring a designer or learning complex software.

use_cases.title: Content marketing and blogging
use_cases.description: Bloggers and content marketers use Snappa to design featured images and graphics that match their brand for articles and campaigns.
